turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Alito has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 17-646, Gamble v. United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: We consider in this case whether to overrule a longstanding interpretation of the Double Jeopardy Clause of the Fifth Amendment. That Clause provides that no person may be 'twice put in jeopardy for the same offense.' At its core the Clause means that a person who is acquitted or convicted of a particular offense cannot be tried a second time for the same offense. But what does the Clause mean by an offense? We have long held, for more than a 100 years, that a crime under one sovereign's laws is not the same offense as a crime under the laws of another sovereign. Under this doctrine, the so-called Dual Sovereignty Doctrine, a state may prosecute a defendant under state law even if the federal government has prosecuted him for the same conduct under a federal statute or the reverse may happen, as it did here. Terance Gamble, who was convicted by Alabama for possessing a firearm as a convicted felon, now faces prosecution by the United States under its own Felon and Possession Law. Attacking the second prosecution on Double Jeopardy grounds, Gamble asked us to overrule the Dual Sovereignty Doctrine and we have painstakingly reexamined the text of the Fifth Amendment evidence on the original understanding of the Double Jeopardy Clause and our prior precedents, and we adhere to the position that we have taken for well over a century. To begin, the text of the Double Jeopardy Clause cuts against Gamble's argument. The Clause prohibits Double Jeopardy for the same offense, not for the same conduct or the same action. And as originally understood, the term offense meant a violation of the law of a particular sovereign, so where there are two sovereigns, there are two laws and therefore there are two offenses, not one offense. This understanding recognizes that different sovereigns can have different interests in punishing the same act. Consider the case of an American national who is murdered abroad, the country in which that crime occurs of course has an interest in prosecuting that murder, just as it has an interest in prosecuting any murder that occurs within its borders. But the United States has independent interests in protecting its own nationals. The centerpiece of Gamble's request that we reconsider the Dual Sovereignty Doctrine is his argument that it was well-established at common law that a person who was prosecuted for crime in a foreign country could not be prosecuted for the same crime in Britain and that the Double Jeopardy Clause was meant to incorporate this rule. Well, after granting review in this case and reading the briefs and hearing the arguments we have looked high and low in search of this established common law rule. We have dusted off 16th and 17th Century English casebooks and old treatises and this search has turned out to be very much like the search for the yeti. What we have is a grainy photograph taken from a long distance, a kind of a dubious footprint in the mud, conflicting stories from unreliable witnesses. The cases that Gamble cited are a muddle as we found when we examined them the last time, the treatises are not much better. It takes a lot more than that to overturn settled precedent. So for these and other reasons set forth in the opinion of the Court, we affirm the Eleventh Circuit's decision rejecting Gamble's Double Jeopardy challenge. Justice Thomas has filed a concurrence. Justices Ginsburg and Gorsuch have each filed dissenting opinions.
